analysis:
- Strong heuristics converge on the same architecture: Pareto (nondominated) filtering → cheap proxy ranking (box/box‑volume, diversity) → small shortlist → greedy selection with exact marginal hypervolume on the shortlist → bounded swap local search. They add practical caps (shortlist size, swap budget) and some use CELF-style lazy re-evaluation to cut HV calls.
- Weaker/expensive designs (Heuristic 1, 2) either use population/EAs or exhaustive per‑candidate HV recomputation without caching/timestamps, causing many redundant HV evaluations and slow convergence.
- Common inefficiencies: repeated construction of pygmo.hypervolume objects, recomputing identical HVs after no-change, non‑vectorized dominance checks, and large candidate pools. Better ones reduce HV calls via proxy upper bounds, caching marginal gains, and early stopping.
- Good tradeoffs seen: modest shortlist (O(k)–O(8k)), robust fallback padding, and small bounded local search yields near‑best HV with far fewer expensive HV evaluations.
experience: Prefer Pareto filter + tight proxy upper bounds + CELF lazy greedy with cached (timestamped) marginal gains, small O(k log n) candidate pools, reuse hypervolume objects or incremental HV updates, vectorize dominance filtering, and cap local search. This minimizes HV calls while keeping HV quality high.
current_reflection: Prefer Pareto filter + tight proxy upper bounds + CELF lazy greedy with cached (timestamped) marginal gains, small O(k log n) candidate pools, reuse hypervolume objects or incremental HV updates, vectorize dominance filtering, and cap local search. This minimizes HV calls while keeping HV quality high.
good_reflections: Prefer a hybrid constructive: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized Monte‑Carlo or fast tensor proxies to shortlist candidates, then apply exact HV only on the shortlist with cached/incremental updates and a small swap-based local search. Vectorize dominance precomputation, avoid rebuilding objects in loops, and keep all heavy work batched.

Next generation heuristics should: (1) pareto‑filter then shortlist by cheap proxy; (2) compute exact marginal HVC only on that shortlist using cached/incremental HV data structures; (3) vectorize or Monte‑Carlo only when needed; (4) cap HV evaluations and local search adaptively; and (5) reuse pygmo objects and add deterministic seeding and parallel shortlist evaluation.
bad_reflections: Combine pareto filtering + a CELF-style lazy greedy seeded by box‑volume (or MC) proxies, cache evaluated gains with timestamps, limit shortlist size to O(k log n), and restrict swap candidates by proxy. Batch/hot-path vectorization and reusing a single hypervolume object cut overhead. Cap local‑search iterations tightly. These steps give near-greedy HV with predictable, low runtime.

Use a Pareto filter + strong box‑volume proxy, then CELF lazy greedy with cached marginal gains and timestamps to avoid re-evaluations. Limit candidate pool adaptively (O(max(k,200))), reuse/avoid rebuilding HV objects, cap Monte‑Carlo samples, and run first‑improvement bounded swaps on a proxy-ranked small pool. Vectorize dominance and distance calculations; profile HV calls and budget them deterministically.

Use pareto filtering + box‑volume proxy to cap candidates, apply CELF lazy greedy with cached marginal gains keyed by (candidate, selection_size), limit shortlist size (∼min(200,8·k)), reuse current HV to compute deltas, and finish with a tiny bounded swap search. Avoid full O(n·k) HV scans and repeated hypervolume-object recreation.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto filter, proxy shortlist (box‑volume/MC/tensor), CELF lazy greedy, cached timestamped marginals, incremental/reused hypervolume, O(k log n) candidate pool, vectorized dominance, bounded local swaps.
- Advice: Pareto‑filter then shortlist by cheap proxy, run CELF with timestamped cached gains, compute exact HV only for shortlist using incremental/reused HV objects, cap shortlist size and local‑search iterations, batch/vectorize heavy work, use deterministic seeds and parallel shortlist evaluation.
- Avoid: rebuilding HV objects, full O(n·k) HV scans, unbounded Monte Carlo or swap loops, unvectorized hot loops, skipping cache/timestamps.
- Explanation: This minimizes expensive HV evaluations while preserving near‑greedy hypervolume quality and giving predictable, tunable runtime.
